content:
Mi spiace ma stai facendo confusione. Non esiste un server di Thunderbird. È Thunderbird che -se configurato correttamente- si collega al server di Gmail/Yahoo/Etc. e scarica sul PC una copia dei messaggi che vi trova.

Detto questo, Per poter inquadrare il problema prova a fornire queste informazioni:

  1. Versione di Thunderbird
  2. Versione del sistema operativo
  3. Provider delle caselle email (Gmail, Libero, Hotmail, etc.)
  4. Tipologia di account (POP3, IMAP)
  5. Estensioni installate
  6. Messaggi di errore che vengono visualizzati durante l'utilizzo di Thunderbird
  7. Software di sicurezza eventualmente presenti (Antivirus e simili)
  8. Screenshot del problema (se utile a chiarire il contesto)
creator: @next

Make sure to allow cookies in Thunderbird. Don't use a VPN when attempting to authenticate. Also make sure there is no local web server running on your computer or any other service listening to TCP port 80. To check this open a command prompt, and run this command:

netstat -an | findstr LISTEN | findstr ":80[^0-9]"

If you are still stuck, please post screenshots illustrating the problem.

content:
Your settings look correct, according to those I found at the infomaniak help site:

https://www.infomaniak.com/en/support/faq/2430/configure-thunderbird-in-imap-mail

https://www.infomaniak.com/en/support/faq/468/understanding-mail-ports-and-protocols

Recommended IMAP settings: Username = the complete & full email address (?) Password = the one created/generated for the email address you want to access Incoming IMAP server = mail.infomaniak.com IMAP port = 993 (with SSL)

Recommended SMTP settings: Username = full & complete email address ( ? ) Password = the one created/generated for the email address you want to access Outgoing SMTP server = mail.infomaniak.com SMTP port & encryption protocol and command/method to activate this protocol: 

   Prioritize the port 587 + STARTTLS: this is the official technical standard and the most compatible method with the majority of modern systems for authenticated submission.    Tolerate the port 465 + SSL/TLS only as a compatibility option or alternative, specifying that it is implicit encryption, but that 587 remains the recommended choice.

Authentication required to send emails: 

   It is activated in the SMTP settings of your application.    Check "use the same parameters as the incoming server" or depending on the software/email clients, enter a username (= full & complete email address) and its password. 
POP3 protocol not recommended (POP server = mail.infomaniak.com and POP port = 995 with SSL).

Are you using the password for your email account, rather than your master infomaniak password?

You should try turning off Thunderbird's "experimental" Account Hub feature and setting the account up manually. That setting is found (in Windows) at < Tools | Settings | General | Account Hub > - uncheck "Create accounts in the new Account Hub."

content:
First, please keep in mind that Thunderbird is free. Being free, it depends on volunteers to assist. Volunteers give of their own time as part of the community assist. Most problems can be resolved if enough information is shared.

Second, I read your prior post. It was lacking any information that may have opened a window on a possible problem. This is because most problems tie in some way to the email host's requirements. You did not state whether IMAP or POP, or the email host's name, or whether there was more information, such as a recent update or restore. Providing more information allows volunteers to assess the possible problem and whether the volunteer has knowledge on that topic.

So, if you will provide some detail on your problem, you may get the support you seek. SO, since I'm here, I'll prompt a bit. Is this IMAP or POP? Did this loss happen with an update? Did you lose all mail or just incoming (or outgoing)? What antivirus do you use? Is this Windows, Linux, or Mac? What version of thunderbird are you using? Knowing this may help. thank you
creator: davidsk

content:
There may be more than one way to accomplish this. Different versions of Outlook Express might be different - I tested with OE6. My method was:

- Create one or more folders to receive the emails exported from Outlook Express. I put my folders on the desktop for convenience.

- Open Outlook Express and open a folder to display the emails. Select all the emails in that folder and drag-and-drop them into one of the folders you created above. This will export the messages to individual .EML files. Repeat the process for all folders.

- Install Thunderbird but decline to set up email accounts during setup. You will be using Local Folders only.

- In the Local Folders section of the Accounts pane create a folder structure to your liking.

- Drag-and-drop the exported EML files onto the Thunderbird folders you created.


There is a Thunderbird add-on called "ImportExportTools NG" that can simplify the import process somewhat.

If you no longer have a working copy of OE, there are a few 3rd-party programs that claim to be able to convert OE DBX folder files to EML format. I can't vouch for any of them.
creator: rednoise
